title: When an array's length changes under an edit, the whole array conflicts — atomically.
why:
Engine spec §4.1, the known-coarse-but-correct rule. The user edited containers[0].image; the server injected a sidecar, so containers now has 2 elements. A positional merge would reconcile ours[0] against theirs[0] and ours[1] (absent) against theirs[1] — and with a PREPENDED element it would silently merge the user's edit into the wrong container.
So: any array that is dirty or whose length moved is treated as ONE value. The user's array wins until they resolve it, and the whole array is the conflict. Coarse — their one-field edit now conflicts with an unrelated append — but never wrong. It also agrees with RFC 7386, which replaces arrays wholesale anyway, so the patch format needs no special case.
The right long-term answer is a keyed merge (x-kubernetes-list-map-keys: containers by `name`), driven by the OpenAPI schema. That is the project's main open design question, not a TODO.

title: A routine watch BOOKMARK is absorbed — never forwarded, never mistaken for `synced`.
why:
Kubernetes, verbatim: "The document representing the BOOKMARK event is of the type requested by the request, but only includes a .metadata.resourceVersion field." So an object with no uid, no name, no spec and no status is not an exotic case someone contrived — it is on EVERY conforming watch stream that asked for bookmarks, and the gateway must ask (allowWatchBookmarks=true is how the snapshot boundary arrives at all).
Two ways to get this wrong, and this fixture fails on both:

  - forward the bookmark's object as `modified`. The consumer REPLACES on modified — that is the
    protocol's single most important rule — so it would replace a live ConfigMap with a husk that
    has only a resourceVersion. The screen goes blank.
  - treat every bookmark as the snapshot boundary and emit `synced`. Pruning is gated on `synced`,
    so a mid-cycle `synced` prunes objects the snapshot had not reached yet: half the user's
    resources vanish, and reappear on the next relist.

Only the bookmark that TERMINATES the initial events is `synced` (spec §5, gateway §3a). Every other one is absorbed for its resourceVersion and never spoken of again (spec §2).
See docs/facts/kubernetes-api-concepts.md §1.1 — this rule is a quote from the API docs, not an opinion about them.

title: A real conflict is raised, kept, and then clears itself when the server converges.
why:
R-DERIVED. Two halves:
(1) The server changed the very key the user is editing, to a different value. Keep the user's
    edit — never silently overwrite what someone typed — and surface what the cluster says.

(2) Then the server's value ARRIVES at what the user typed (a colleague made the same change).
    The conflict must clear, and the field must stop being dirty — draft == server now. This is
    the half a cached `dirty` set gets wrong: it goes stale after a watch event and the field
    stays flagged forever, or only unflags itself on the next click.
    Dirtiness is DERIVED — deepEqual(draft@path, base@path) — never remembered.

title: Delete and recreate under the same name — new uid, no state bleeds across.
why: Identity is metadata.uid, never metadata.name. A consumer keyed on name would merge the new object's state into the old one's draft and quietly show a mixture of two different objects. Note the user had a pending edit on the OLD object: it dies with it. That is correct — the object they were editing no longer exists.

title: A label key containing dots and a slash is ONE path segment.
why:
R-ID. `app.kubernetes.io/name` dot-joined into a path string becomes "metadata.labels.app.kubernetes.io/name", which splits back into SIX segments and addresses nothing. The symptom is the worst kind: no crash, no error — the conflict lookup simply never matches, so conflicts silently never appear and the user's edit is silently lost on save.
Paths are ARRAYS of segments. Where a scalar key is unavoidable (a conflict map), encode the path structurally (JSON.stringify of the array) — never by joining with a magic separator.

title: A server change to a key you are NOT editing must not disturb the key you ARE.
why:
R-THREEWAY. The bug this defends against: comparing the incoming server object to the DRAFT instead of to the PREVIOUS SERVER OBJECT. Do that, and every watch event looks like "the server changed everything the user typed" — so an unrelated `replicas` bump falsely conflicts a `log-level` edit, and the console fights the user on every controller heartbeat.
The base is the previous server state. If base == theirs for a path, the server did not move it, and the draft is left ALONE — no follow, no flash, no conflict.

title: A map key deleted upstream disappears from the draft — while an in-flight edit survives.
why:
The removal case at map level, and it is subtler than it looks. The server deleted `data.replicas` and the `note` annotation. The user is meanwhile editing `data.log-level`.
Three things must happen at once, and each is a different rule:
  - `data.replicas`     -> gone from the draft. The user never touched it, so it follows the
                           server, and "follows the server" includes following it into deletion.
                           (A UI must drop the row — this is the `structural` signal.)
  - `metadata.annotations.note` -> likewise gone.
  - `data.log-level`    -> untouched by the server, so the edit stands, dirty, unconflicted.

A consumer that deep-merges leaves BOTH deleted keys on screen as ghosts the user can still type into — and then a save resurrects them in the cluster.

title: A named scope whose object does not exist is still framed reset…synced — not silence.
why: This is the fixture that kills the tempting "named scopes may skip the snapshot" optimization. If the object was deleted while the consumer was away, a reconnect that emits nothing leaves the old uid on screen as a ghost FOREVER — there is no event that ever removes it. One framing, one consumer state machine, both scope types.

title: A field the server removed must disappear — replacement, not deep-merge.
why:
The single most important consumer rule, and the one an "obvious" implementation gets wrong. A generic deep-merge of the incoming object over the previous one CANNOT express a removal:

    old: { spec: { replicas: 2, paused: false } }
    new: { spec: { replicas: 2 } }              <- server removed `paused`
    deep-merge -> { replicas: 2, paused: false }   WRONG: a ghost, in a nested subtree
    replace    -> { replicas: 2 }                  RIGHT

The authoritative server object is REPLACED wholesale. The three-way merge with local edits is layered ABOVE that — never in place of it.

title: A snapshot cycle that never reaches synced prunes nothing.
why: The consumer is fed reset + one added, and then the connection dies mid-cycle. If pruning were driven by "reset clears the map" instead of "synced prunes the unseen", the user would watch half their resources evaporate every time the network hiccuped. State from BEFORE the reset survives until a cycle completes.

title: A metadata-only object is never forwarded as an upsert — the gateway resnapshots instead.
why:
Kubernetes serves PartialObjectMetadata on request (Accept: application/json;as=PartialObjectMetadata): "the returned objects only contain the `metadata` field. The `spec` and `status` fields are omitted."
This fixture exists because it caught a real mistake in our own gateway. The guard was "an object with no uid is partial" — but a PartialObjectMetadata HAS a uid. It has a whole metadata block. What it does not have is a spec or a status. So the guard looked at the wrong field, the object sailed through, and the consumer — whose model is REPLACE, never merge — swapped a live Deployment for a husk: the status view goes blank and the editor silently loses the user's spec.
The honest check is the KIND (PartialObjectMetadata / PartialObjectMetadataList, group meta.k8s.io), not the presence of a uid. A partial object delivered as an update would blank a consumer's state — spec §2 says so, and it is right; we were simply not enforcing what we had written down.
The recovery is a new snapshot cycle: it is the one response that is ALWAYS correct, because it re-establishes the truth rather than guessing at it.

title: An object deleted while the consumer was disconnected is pruned on the next synced.
why: No persistent ghosts. The consumer never saw the `deleted` event — it was offline — so the ONLY thing that can remove feature-flags is the reset…synced boundary: mark all unseen on reset, seen on each added, prune what is still unseen on synced.

title: A 40-digit resourceVersion orders correctly — and a stale replay is still dropped.
why:
Kubernetes: "Resource versions are compared as arbitrary bitsize decimal integers... The bitsize must not be assumed to be some fixed amount." Its own worked example is 40 digits long.
strconv.ParseInt tops out at 19. The gateway used to compare resource versions with it, so against a server like this one the parse simply failed — and the per-object monotonicity check (spec §6) gave up. The user-visible symptom is silently dropped live updates, which in a status view is indistinguishable from "Kubernetes is being slow", and is therefore the kind of bug that survives for years.
Note the KIND. This is a Flunder — Kubernetes' own sample aggregated API — and not a ConfigMap, on purpose: kube-apiserver's resourceVersion is an etcd revision, so it fits in an int64 and you will never meet a 40-digit one there. A server with a different backing store is where such a value actually comes from, and a fixture that pretended otherwise would be teaching the rule with an example that cannot happen.
The comparison Kubernetes prescribes: "If they are not of equal length, the longer one is greater... If they are of equal length, the lexicographically greater one is greater." That rules out a plain lexicographic compare, which would call the 41-digit version OLDER than the 40-digit one.
So, both halves at once:
  - the 41-digit v2 is NEWER than the 40-digit v1 and must be delivered;
  - replaying v1 afterwards is STALE and must be dropped (§6: never emit, within a cycle, a state
    older than one already emitted for that uid).

These are decimals, so they ARE orderable: the default (OrderingStrict) handles them, and must.

title: An aggregated API's unorderable resourceVersion is refused, loudly — not tolerated in silence.
why:
Kubernetes 1.35 made orderability a CONFORMANCE requirement, for built-in types and custom resources alike. This library REQUIRES 1.35+ and relies on it: a ConfigMap, a Deployment or a CRD cannot serve an unorderable resourceVersion, so a fixture using one would be defending against a case a conformant cluster cannot produce.
The one server that still may is an AGGREGATED / extension API server — a third-party implementation the conformance test does not cover. Hence a Flunder (wardle.example.com), Kubernetes' own sample aggregated API. The docs' carve-out is written for exactly this: "if either of two resource version strings does not parse as a decimal number, the two strings can be checked for equality but you cannot rely on comparisons for ordering."
The protocol promises consumers per-object monotonicity (§6) — within a cycle you are never handed a state older than one you already hold — and the gateway keeps that promise BY ordering resource versions. If it cannot order them, it cannot keep it. A gateway that quietly carries on anyway leaves a consumer believing a guarantee it is no longer getting, which is strictly worse than one that has been told.
So the default (Gateway.Ordering = OrderingStrict) refuses: a terminal INTERNAL error that NAMES the escape hatch, so the operator of an aggregated API is never merely stuck. OrderingLenient streams it anyway and simply stops ordering — dropping nothing it cannot compare, because a duplicate is harmless (apply is idempotent) while a wrongly-dropped update is data loss.
Note WHERE the refusal lands: after `reset`, because the gateway only learns what this upstream's resource versions look like when the first object arrives. It cannot know from the scope alone.

title: Upstream continuity can be lost WITHOUT the SSE connection dropping — a fresh cycle follows.
why: 410 Gone, an informer relist, a cache reset. The SSE connection is perfectly healthy, but the gateway can no longer promise it saw every change — so it must begin a new snapshot cycle rather than silently continue. A gateway that just relays the watch cannot do this, and the ghost it leaves is invisible until someone notices a deleted object still on screen. It SHOULD announce the break first with a non-terminal RESYNC_REQUIRED.

title: A redacted value is not on the wire at all — the key is disclosed, the value is withheld.
why:
Keys-only disclosure, and the reason it is now SAFE by construction rather than by discipline.
The projection used to substitute a mask — `**REDACTED**` — for a Secret's value. That mask was the only poisoned value in the system, and we invented it: a browser holding it can send it back on an ordinary save, and the literal string `**REDACTED**` is written OVER the real secret. The token is destroyed, and from the browser it looked like a green tick. It needed a guard on every adopter's save endpoint, forever, correctly (proposal 0003).
So we stopped inventing it. Two obligations now, and neither is belt-and-braces:

  1. the gateway DELETES the value and declares the path (RFC 6901 pointers) in `redacted` —
     on the wire, per object, mandatory, authoritative. That array is where the consumer learns
     `token` exists at all, and it carries everything the mask used to carry;
  2. the client marks those paths read-only: no draft, no dirty, no conflict, never in a patch.

The third obligation — "the server must reject a patch touching a redacted path" — is GONE, because the thing it defended against cannot happen: there is no redacted value anywhere for a draft to hold or a patch to carry.
Editing an UNREDACTED field of the same object must still work — that is the whole point of keys-only disclosure. You can see that `token` exists, you can rename the object's labels, and you can never learn or clobber the token itself.

title: The basic cycle — snapshot first, then live deltas.
why: reset … added* … synced, and only then live events. The snapshot exists so the browser paints immediately rather than waiting for something to change; the watch resumes from the list's resourceVersion, so no event between the two is lost.

title: Status follows the server live while an edit survives.
why:
`krm-full/v1` keeps `status`, read-only and live. A controller-only update must flash and follow the server while an in-flight edit to `spec.replicas` survives untouched and remains the only patchable change.
This is the browser demo: watch Kubernetes reconcile while you type.

title: A status-only update produces no event when the selected projection ignores status.
why:
`krm-spec/v1` ignores `status`, so a controller-only update must emit no event at all. Meanwhile the user is editing `spec.replicas`; the suppressed status heartbeat must not touch that draft.
On a real workload this — not editing — is the dominant traffic on the stream.

title: An ambiguous tombstone is never emitted as `deleted` — a new cycle prunes instead.
why:
spec §4.2, and it is stated in strong terms for a reason: if the gateway cannot recover a trustworthy uid it MUST NOT emit an ambiguous `deleted`. It begins a new snapshot cycle and lets reset…synced do the pruning.
The source of a degenerate tombstone is client-go's cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown: the informer missed the delete and noticed during a relist, so it hands you a wrapper whose object may be nothing more than a key. A gateway that "helpfully" reconstructs a uid from the name deletes THE WRONG OBJECT out of somebody's browser — and a delete-and-recreate under the same name (see delete-recreate-uid) is exactly the case where the name is a lie.
Paying for a relist is the cheap option here. Guessing is not.
